title: Best Herbal Remedies for Jet Lag and Fatigue
intro_desc: Herbal teas for jet lag are a type of natural remedy that helps alleviate symptoms associated with jet lag, such as fatigue, insomnia, and disrupted circadian rhythms. These teas are particularly effective due to their adaptogenic properties, which enable the body to adjust to new time zones. The best herbal teas for jet lag include Valeriana officinalis, Melissa officinalis, Passiflora incarnata, Lavandula angustifolia, Camellia sinensis, Zingiber officinale, Ginkgo biloba, Avena sativa, Glycyrrhiza glabra, and Corydalis yanhusuo. If left untreated, jet lag can significantly impact daily life, leading to sleep deprivation, decreased productivity, and mood disturbances, ultimately affecting relationships, work performance, and overall well-being.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis tea, also known as valerian root tea, is a natural remedy that can help with jet lag. The tea is made from the roots of the valerian plant, which contains a chemical that helps people fall asleep and stay asleep. When you're traveling across time zones, your body's internal clock gets confused, making it hard to sleep. Valerian root tea can calm your body and mind, making it easier to fall asleep at your new time zone. It also helps to reduce stress and anxiety that can come with traveling. This is because the chemical in valerian root, called valerenic acid, acts as a sedative, making it easier to relax and fall asleep. Drinking valerian root tea before bedtime can help your body adjust to the new time zone faster, reducing the symptoms of jet lag.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Valerenic acid is good for jet lag because it helps to adjust body clocks and improve sleep quality by reducing stress and promoting relaxation.

Valeranone is good for jet lag because it helps regulate sleep patterns by promoting relaxation and reducing stress caused by traveling across different time zones, making it easier for people to adjust to new sleep schedules.
Valerenal is good for jet lag because it helps regulate sleep patterns by reducing anxiety and stress, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep after traveling across time zones.

remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal teas for jet lag
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula latifolia herbal tea that helps with jet lag, start by gathering dried lavender flowers and boiling water. Begin by measuring one teaspoon of dried Lavandula latifolia flowers for every 8 ounces of water, and place them in a teapot or infuser. Next, boil fresh water and pour it over the lavender flowers, letting them steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the herbal properties to release. While the tea steeps, prepare a warm and comfortable environment to help your body relax and adjust to the new time zone. Once the tea has steeped, strain the flowers and discard them, then add honey or lemon to taste if desired. After drinking the tea, try to relax and get some rest to help your body adjust to the new time zone and reduce the effects of jet lag.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia tea, also known as English lavender tea, is a great help for people who travel across different time zones. This is because it contains chemicals called flavonoids and terpenes, which can help calm the body and mind. When you travel, your body gets confused because its internal clock is not in sync with the new location's time. This can cause problems like insomnia, fatigue, and stomach upset. Lavandula angustifolia tea can help your body adjust to the new time zone by promoting relaxation and reducing stress levels. It also helps regulate your sleep patterns, which is essential for recovering from jet lag. Drinking this tea in the evening can signal to your body that it's time to sleep, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep. Additionally, the tea's calming effects can also help reduce anxiety and irritability that often come with traveling.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Linalool is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock by promoting relaxation and reducing stress, which can disrupt sleep patterns and contribute to symptoms of jet lag.
Linalyl acetate is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate sleep patterns and reduce stress caused by time zone changes.
Apigenin is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate body rhythms and improve sleep quality by reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation.

remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal teas for jet lag
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula latifolia herbal tea that helps with jet lag, start by gathering dried lavender flowers, which are the main ingredient. You can find dried lavender flowers at most health food stores or online. Next, boil fresh water in a kettle or pot, and then add one teaspoon of the dried lavender flowers for every cup of water. Let the flowers steep in the boiling water for about 5-7 minutes, or until the water has cooled slightly. After the steeping time has passed, strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the lavender flowers. The tea is now ready to drink. To help with jet lag, drink a cup of the lavender tea about 30 minutes before bedtime. The soothing properties of lavender can help calm your body and mind, making it easier to fall asleep, which is especially helpful when traveling across time zones.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis tea, also known as lemon balm tea, can help with jet lag. This is because it helps your body adjust to the new time zone. The tea contains a chemical called rosmarinic acid, which makes you feel more relaxed and calm. When you're on a plane, the stress and anxiety from the flight can make it harder to adjust to the new time zone. The rosmarinic acid in lemon balm tea helps reduce this stress and anxiety, making it easier for your body to adjust to the new time zone. Melissa officinalis tea also helps regulate your body's internal clock. The tea contains a chemical called melatonin, which is a natural sleep aid. When you're on a plane, it's easy to get out of your normal sleep schedule. The melatonin in lemon balm tea helps regulate your body's internal clock, making it easier for you to fall asleep and stay asleep when you need to. Additionally, Melissa officinalis tea has been shown to improve the quality of sleep. When you're on a plane, it's easy to get a poor night's sleep due to the noise, discomfort, and stress of the flight. The tea's rosmarinic acid and melatonin help improve the quality of sleep, making it easier for you to get the rest you need when you arrive at your destination. Finally, Melissa officinalis tea can help reduce jet lag symptoms such as headaches and fatigue. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties can help reduce the inflammation that can cause headaches, and its antioxidant properties can help reduce fatigue. By reducing these symptoms, the tea can make it easier for you to adjust to the new time zone and enjoy your trip.

Begin by boiling water in a kettle, then reduce the heat to a simmer. Next, add a teaspoon of dried Lavender flowers to a tea infuser or a small strainer, and place it in a cup. Pour the hot water over the Lavender flowers, and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. As the tea steeps, the heat from the water will release the herbal properties of the Lavender, creating a soothing and calming brew. Once the tea has steeped, remove the infuser or strainer, and your Lavandula latifolia herbal tea is ready to drink. To help with jet lag, it is recommended to drink this tea in the evening, as it will help promote relaxation and a good night's sleep, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale tea, also known as ginger tea, is a good remedy for jet lag. This is because ginger has properties that help to reduce nausea and vomiting, which are common symptoms of jet lag. When you travel across different time zones, your body's internal clock gets confused, leading to these symptoms. Ginger helps to calm your stomach and prevent these feelings of sickness. Additionally, ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce fatigue and tiredness caused by jet lag. It also contains antioxidants that help to protect your body from the negative effects of travel, such as oxidative stress. Drinking ginger tea can help to regulate your body's natural rhythms and reduce the severity of jet lag symptoms. It's especially useful when consumed in the morning, as it can help to boost your energy levels and get you feeling more alert.

remedies.remedy_desc: Avena sativa tea, also known as oats tea, is a natural remedy for jet lag. This tea is made from the dried leaves of the oat plant and contains a compound called avenacoside. Avena sativa tea helps to regulate our body's internal clock by promoting relaxation and reducing stress hormones like cortisol. Drinking Avena sativa tea before bedtime can help to improve the quality of sleep, making it easier to adjust to the new time zone. Drinking Avena sativa tea may help to alleviate some of the discomfort caused by jet lag, making it easier to adapt to new time zones.

Additionally, ginkgo biloba tea has been shown to help regulate sleep patterns and improve the quality of sleep. remedies.remedy_desc: Camellia sinensis tea, also known as green tea, is a good choice to help with jet lag because it contains an amino acid called L-theanine. This helps the body relax and reduces stress. When you travel to a different time zone, your body gets confused and it's hard to fall asleep at night and stay awake during the day. L-theanine in green tea helps you feel more calm and relaxed, making it easier to fall asleep when you need to. It also helps your body adjust to the new time zone by regulating your sleep patterns. Green tea also contains caffeine, which can help you feel more alert and awake during the day, but in a natural way. This can help you feel more energized and focused, making it easier to adjust to your new surroundings. Drinking green tea can also help your body recover from the fatigue and stress of traveling, making it a great choice for people who experience jet lag.

remedies.image_alt: Lavandula latifolia herbal teas for jet lag
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Lavandula latifolia herbal tea, also known as English lavender tea, which can help with jet lag, start by gathering fresh or dried lavender flowers. Choose high-quality, organic flowers for the best results. Next, measure out one teaspoon of dried flowers or three tablespoons of fresh flowers for every eight ounces of boiling water. Pour the boiling water over the lavender flowers in a teapot or a heat-resistant cup, and let it steep for five to seven minutes. Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the flowers. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to use a small amount as lavender can be quite potent. Once the tea has cooled slightly, drink it before bedtime to help regulate your sleep-wake cycle and alleviate jet lag symptoms. It's recommended to drink the tea for a few days after travel to adjust to the new time zone.

supplementary_best: A combination of Valeriana officinalis, Lavandula angustifolia, and Avena sativa can help remedy jet lag. Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian root, is a natural sedative that promotes relaxation and reduces stress caused by traveling. It helps regulate the body's internal clock and improves sleep quality. Lavandula angustifolia, or lavender, is a calming herb that eases anxiety and promotes a sense of calmness. It also helps reduce muscle tension, which is common during travel. Avena sativa, or oat straw, is known for its ability to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce stress. It also helps improve mood and reduce irritability. This combination works well because it addresses the two main issues caused by jet lag: stress and sleep disturbances. The valerian root helps with sleep, while the lavender calms the mind, and the oat straw regulates the body's internal clock.
supplementary_avoid: If you have jet lag, there are some herbal teas you should avoid. One of them is 'Ephedra sinica', also known as Ma Huang. It's a stimulant that can increase alertness, but it can also worsen insomnia and make it harder to sleep. This is the opposite of what you need when you're trying to adjust to a new time zone.  Another tea to avoid is 'Yohimbe bark', which is a natural stimulant. It can increase heart rate and blood pressure, making it difficult to relax and fall asleep. This can make jet lag symptoms worse.  You should also avoid 'Guarana seeds' tea, which is high in caffeine. While a small amount of caffeine can be helpful in the morning, too much can disrupt sleep patterns and make it harder to adjust to a new time zone.  'Kola nut' tea is another herbal tea to avoid when you have jet lag. It contains caffeine and other stimulants that can interfere with sleep.  Lastly, 'Yerba mate' tea is high in caffeine and can have a stimulating effect. Drinking it in the evening can make it harder to fall asleep and worsen jet lag symptoms.
supplementary_other_remedies: When we travel across time zones, our bodies have trouble adjusting to the new schedule. One natural remedy that can help is getting some morning sunlight. Exposure to natural light in the morning helps regulate our circadian rhythms, which are like internal clocks that tell our body when to be awake and when to sleep. This is because sunlight triggers the release of a hormone called melatonin, which makes us feel sleepy, and another hormone called serotonin, which helps us feel awake. By exposing ourselves to sunlight in the morning, we can trick our body into thinking it's morning, even if it's still night outside.  Another way to help our body adjust is by staying hydrated. Dehydration can make us feel tired and groggy, which can make jet lag worse. Drinking plenty of water before and after our trip can help prevent dehydration. We can also try to stay hydrated by eating hydrating foods like watermelon and cucumbers. These foods are made up of mostly water, which can help keep our body hydrated.  Getting some exercise can also help our body adjust to a new time zone. Exercise can help regulate our circadian rhythms by releasing hormones that help us feel awake and alert. It can also help us feel more energized and focused, which can make it easier to adjust to a new time zone. However, it's best to exercise in the morning, as exercising in the evening can make it harder to fall asleep.  Taking a warm bath before bed can also help us adjust to a new time zone. A warm bath can help relax our body and mind, making it easier to fall asleep. The warm water can also help increase the release of melatonin, which makes us feel sleepy. This can help us fall asleep faster and get a good night's rest, which is essential for adjusting to a new time zone.  Lastly, trying to maintain a consistent sleep schedule can also help our body adjust to a new time zone. This means going to bed and waking up at the same time every day, even on weekends. This can help our body get into a routine, which can make it easier to adjust to a new time zone.
supplementary_related_ailments: People who have jet lag often experience more than just tiredness and confusion. They may also feel pain in their muscles and joints, which can be due to the body's stress response to the time change. Medicinal herbs like ginger and turmeric can help with this pain. Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties, which reduce swelling and pain in the body. Turmeric, on the other hand, contains a compound called curcumin, which also has anti-inflammatory properties and can help with pain relief.  Another common symptom of jet lag is digestive problems, such as constipation, diarrhea, or stomach cramps. Peppermint oil and licorice root can help with these issues. Peppermint oil can calm the stomach and prevent nausea, while licorice root has anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe the digestive system and prevent stomach cramps.  Many people who experience jet lag also have trouble sleeping, which can be due to the body's confusion about the time of day. Valerian root is a natural herb that can help with sleep. It has a calming effect on the body and can help people fall asleep faster and sleep more soundly.  Jet lag can also cause fatigue, which can be due to the body's stress response and the lack of sleep. Ashwagandha is an herbal supplement that can help with fatigue. It is known to reduce stress and anxiety, which can help people feel more energized and focused.  Finally, some people may experience headaches and migraines due to the change in environment and time. Feverfew is a medicinal herb that can help with headaches and migraines. It has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce pain and inflammation in the body.
